Write the field guide for
your corner of the field
CLARK field guides — a call for domain collaborators
Sha Liu · github.com/Shalice-in-AIland/claude-language-assessment-research-kit
1

A citable output — not a favour

Each released guide carries its curator byline, a version, and its own archived DOI — cited like the publication it is. Editorial review before merge: the same propose-and-approve discipline the skills run on.
CLARK — free · open · citable
DOI 10.5281/zenodo.21411273 · MIT
Contact: Sha Liu
2

The skills know how — your field guide knows what

CLARK is an open, citable research kit (7 skills: literature radar → citation integrity → pre-submission review) that automates the mechanical layer of research — and deliberately ships only a backbone of domain specifics.
A field guide is the specifics layer for one domain, curated by a named expert: the venues and what they tell reviewers, construct-specific criteria, controlled vocabularies, the watch-lists a radar sweeps.
Drop a guide into a project and every skill becomes domain-fluent — without a single change to the machinery.
Rater training · speaking · young learners · item writing · listening · LSP … each needs its expert.
3

Three ways in — join at whichever rung fits; climb when you like

Inside a guide — the template, worked for AWE (the founding guide, in preparation):
Key glossary — the field's controlled vocabulary
The venues — and what each tells reviewers
Evaluation criteria — construct-specific, traced to sources
Status quo & key papers — what to read first
Quality checklists — for a living review
Watch-lists — what the radar sweeps
Most of it is the lists you already keep in your head.
The ask: one conversation. Name your domain — we take it from there.
4
